items.q: Which auxiliary does 'gehen' (to go) take in the perfect tense?
items.why: Students default to haben (English 'have'). Verbs of motion or change of state take SEIN (gehen, kommen, laufen, werden). Most other verbs take haben. 'Ich habe gegangen' is incorrect.

A devastating faux ami. Bekommen looks like 'become' but means RECEIVE. 'Ich bekomme ein Geschenk' = I receive a present. To become = WERDEN. Confusion: 'I became a present' instead of 'I received a present'.

items.q: Which relative pronoun do you use for 'the man WHOM I saw' in German?
items.why: Students don't decline relative pronouns. The relative pronoun takes the case its FUNCTION REQUIRES in its clause. 'Whom I saw' makes it the direct object, so accusative: 'Der Mann, DEN ich gesehen habe.'
Correct answer: Aunt. “Uncle” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Big/grand. “Small” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many extended family terms in this lesson?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
